plan:
- Microcontrollers: 2x ESP32 boards for processing and IoT connectivity.
- Sensors: 2x ACS712 current sensors (e.g., 20A or 30A module) for grid and house monitoring.
- Actuators/Outputs: 1x Single-channel relay module (e.g., 5V, 10A/250VAC) for power cutoff, 1x small buzzer for audible alert, 1x 16x2 or 20x4 LCD display for visual feedback.
- Loads/Simulators: 2x Yellow 100-watt incandescent bulbs (one for authorized load, one for bypass theft simulation).
- Power Supply: Appropriate 5V DC power supply for ESP32s and sensors, wiring for mains voltage handling (e.g., terminal blocks, insulated wires).
- Mechanical/Structural: Two tower structures (e.g., acrylic, wood, or aluminum profiles) to represent grid and house, small enclosure for bypass switch.

originalPrompt:
Spark Safe Stream is an active IoT-based power theft detection system designed to monitor grid supply against authorized loads, making it a perfect, high-impact demonstration for the Smart India Hackathon.
​Here is how the project works conceptually and physically:
​The Core Concept
The system relies on a simple law of physics: the electrical current leaving the grid must exactly match the current arriving at the authorized destination. To demonstrate this, the prototype uses a two-tower structure. Tower A represents the utility grid (the source), equipped with an ESP32 microcontroller, an ACS712 current sensor, a relay module, and an LCD display. Tower B represents the authorized household (the destination), wired with a second ACS712 current sensor and a glowing yellow 100-watt bulb.
​Normal Operation
Under normal conditions, the current sensor at the grid source reads the exact same power draw as the sensor at the house. The LCD display continuously updates with real-time power readings, showing matching values (e.g., "Grid: 100W | House: 100W | Status: SECURE").
​Theft Detection and Intervention
Between the two towers is an unauthorized "bypass" wire connected to a second yellow 100-watt bulb. When a manual switch is flipped to turn on this bypass bulb, it simulates an illegal line tap—drawing electricity directly from the grid before it reaches the house's official meter.
​Instantly, the grid sensor (Tower A) detects a spike in current, while the house sensor (Tower B) remains unchanged. The ESP32 calculates this mathematical mismatch. The moment the current differential crosses the allowed threshold, the system takes automated action:
​Visual Alert: The LCD screen immediately updates to read "THEFT DETECTED" and displays the exact amount of unauthorized power being drawn.
​Audible Alarm: The local buzzer sounds a rapid warning to act as a physical deterrent.
​Grid Protection: The ESP32 triggers the relay to physically cut the power line, instantly turning off the yellow bulbs and stopping the active theft.
​Cloud Logging: The event data (including the time and power differential) is pushed to an IoT dashboard so the utility company can investigate the exact node that was compromised.
​Instead of passively waiting for a monthly billing discrepancy to realize energy was stolen, this setup proves how a smart grid can catch a theft in real-time and shut it down automatically.

subSteps.title: 3D print all component mounts and enclosures
Prepare mechanical supports and base plates
Install bypass switch into its 3D printed mount
Install LCD Display into its 3D printed bezel
Install other electrical components into their respective 3D printed mounts
Wire 5V DC power supply to both ESP32 boards
Connect Grid Current Sensor to Grid Controller ESP32
Connect House Current Sensor to House Monitor ESP32
Connect Power Cutoff Relay, Buzzer, and LCD to Grid Controller ESP32
Wire AC input terminal to Grid Current Sensor and Neutral AC wire
Wire Grid Current Sensor output to Power Cutoff Relay and Bypass Switch
Wire Power Cutoff Relay to House Current Sensor and then to Authorized AC Bulb
Wire Bypass Switch to Bypass AC Bulb
Upload firmware to Grid Controller ESP32
Upload firmware to House Monitor ESP32
Verify I2C communication with LCD Display
Test Power Cutoff Relay and Buzzer functionality
Calibrate and verify Grid and House Current Sensor readings
Test Bypass Switch input detection on Grid Controller
Perform initial power-up with Authorized and Bypass bulbs, test theft detection and cutoff logic
Assemble Tower A structure (base plate and vertical supports)
Assemble Tower B structure (base plate and vertical supports)
Mount all Grid-related components and their mounts to Tower A supports
Mount all House-related components and their mounts to Tower B supports
Mount the bypass switch mount and bypass bulb holder into the bypass enclosure
Secure all wiring with cable ties, ensuring strain relief and safety for AC lines
Screw in Authorized AC Bulb and Bypass AC Bulb
Perform final functional test of the complete system

projectDescription: The "Spark Safe Stream" system detects power theft using ESP32 microcontrollers and current sensors to compare grid and household consumption in real-time. It features automated power cutoff via a relay, an audible alarm, and cloud logging of events.

tools: Crimping tool (optional, for ferrules on AC wires)
Computer with Arduino IDE / PlatformIO and USB-C cable
assumptions: Basic understanding of mains AC electricity safety precautions
Familiarity with ESP32 firmware uploading (Arduino IDE or PlatformIO)
Basic electronics knowledge (e.g., distinguishing VCC, GND, data pins)
Access to appropriate 3D printing filament (PLA, PETG)

electricalConnections.label: Grid Sensor Live Out to Relay (Normal Path)
Grid Sensor Live Out to Bypass Switch (Theft Path)
House Sensor Live Out to Authorized Bulb
Authorized Bulb Neutral to Grid Neutral
Bypass Switch Out to Bypass Bulb
Bypass Bulb Neutral to Grid Neutral
